steps.why: The IndexDir implementation shows a runtime table exposed as an immutable directory.
The examples recipe exercises table-backed feature-to-specimen lookup.
The backend row owns reusable Table and Dir implementations.
The install test checks a loadable override table backend.

title: Add a Table or Dir backend

steps.why: Start with the shared codec contract and position rules.
The codec row owns shared positions, limits, and loadable codec contracts.
Expression syntax rows expose language and grammar subjects with round-trip contracts.
Operator-shaped languages reuse the Pratt syntax surface.
The Bridge packet codec owns the Bridge wire grammar.
The wire grammar row groups packet and byte-level protocol contracts.
The Bridge packet codec owns the Bridge wire grammar.
The core row owns read policy, capability checks, and read-construct evidence.
The read-construct test checks object round trips through standard core.
The citizen row owns runtime members and read-construct projection.
The conformance test checks citizen round-trip behavior.
The cookbook row owns reusable recipe metadata.
The EvalFabric row owns runner and fleet placement libraries.
The xtask documentation row owns generated doc lanes and index fragments.
The exec row keeps host process behavior in a loadable library.
Host primitives start from shared network and surface-card helpers.
The host storage row points to file, database, and HTTP backends.
The workflow row ties agent model exchange to Bridge packet records.
The number row owns reusable number domains and their runtime libraries.
The tensor citizen test checks runtime projection for a number domain.
The organ row groups loadable binding, control, logic, pattern, mutation, and sequence behavior.
The Shape row owns matching, binding, and citizen projection.
The inventory test checks the exported shape citizen surface.
The stream row owns reusable stream, rank, topology, and device helpers.
The read-construct test checks stream metadata projection.
The table core row owns shared Table and Dir contracts.

title: Add a Table or Dir core contract

steps.why: The value row owns shared runtime value conversion helpers.
The graph row collects the DSP and plugin recipes for local construction.
The provider row selects a modeled or host-backed audio target.
The copy-node recipe is the smallest graph example.
The vocabulary row supplies pitch and sound descriptors.
The MIDI row moves material into and out of performance formats.
The synth row turns that material into local or browser-backed sound.
The preset recipe is the smallest synth example.
The index row owns the generated fragment.
The runtime command reads the merged index.
The recipe exercises package metadata.
The Forge row owns checked intent compilation.
Server tool rows expose the host-facing model gateway.
The sim-lib-view row owns the default view and reversible edit surfaces.
Device profiles show how projection adapts to concrete surface caps.
The web shell row shows how a browser host loads those surfaces.
The expression syntax row carries language and grammar subjects.
The domain syntax row carries data, document, config, and packet syntax contracts.
The wire row carries packet and byte-level grammar contracts.
The bootloader owns command dispatch into loaded libraries.
The index trace command resolves command surfaces to owning crates and anchors.
The checked trace recipe follows a CLI surface back to code facts.
The loading row owns standard and language-profile runtime libraries.
The web shell row owns the serve, browse, and atelier commands.
The view row supplies the reversible surfaces shown by the browser host.
The open-workspace recipe is the shortest browser-host example.
The Pratt codec is the operator-language parser surface.
The command row owns the operational entry point.
The balanced-year recipe shows the expected yearly voucher shape.
The repair command row owns the operational correction path.
The repair recipe provides the worked contract fixture.
The EvalFabric row owns the stream-fabric placement library.
The fixture command row exposes replay from the CLI surface.
The content-addressed fleet recipe is the replay example.
The codec row chooses the file format and round-trip path.
The surface row renders the document into a reviewable view.
The site row connects the document to modeled workplace sources.
The suite-surface recipe is the shortest surface example.
The MCP row owns the command surface and loaded server libraries.
The stdio bootloader recipe shows the server entry point.
The bootloader prepares the command runtime.
The REPL is the interactive entry point.
The first recipe confirms the command path.
The facade row owns the public command and reversible view surface.
The device row gives wearable workflows through facade-level recipes.
The boot-runtime recipe checks the smallest facade path.
